chunk_summary: This section evaluates expected growth velocity and pubertal tempo in school-age children and adolescents, focusing on stunting and chronic undernutrition as determinants of linear growth failure.
learning_objectives: Understand the expected growth velocity during school-age and adolescence.
Evaluate the impact of chronic undernutrition on pubertal development and linear growth.
Interpret growth reference data for ages 5–19 years using standardized tools.
Identify clinical challenges in diagnosing and managing short stature in children.
key_facts: Normal growth velocity during adolescence ranges from 5–10 cm/year, depending on pubertal stage (Merck Manual).
Pubertal tempo varies significantly, with early puberty linked to faster growth spurts but shorter adult height (NCBI).
WHO growth reference data provides standardized percentiles for ages 5–19 years to assess growth deviations.
Chronic undernutrition is a leading cause of stunting, affecting up to 22% of children globally (WHO).
Short stature in children may require evaluation of growth hormone levels and bone age (Stony Brook Medicine).
reference_quote: Normal growth velocity during adolescence is typically 5–10 cm/year, with variations based on pubertal stage and nutritional status. (Merck Manual)
document_key_topics: Growth velocity in school-age children and adolescents
Pubertal tempo and its impact on growth
